one_line_takeaway: CTFExplorer shows that when LLM offensive agents face 40 undifferentiated web targets at once instead of one known target, models split sharply into high-precision/low-recall conservative exploiters (GPT 5.2, DeepSeek V4 Pro at 100% precision but <=20% recall) versus a high-recall broad explorer (Gemini 3 Pro at 27.50% recall, 64.50% exploration efficiency), and even the best models complete only a fraction of multi-stage kill chains that require pivoting from public access to hidden internal services.
key_finding: On the full 40-target benchmark (Table 4): Gemini 3 Pro found the most flags (13/40, 11/13 correct, 2/13 wrong) and had the highest recall (27.50%) while resolving all 40/40 entry points; Claude Opus 4.5 found 8/40 flags (7/8 correct) with 87.50% precision but resolved only 31/40 entry points; GPT 5.2 and DeepSeek V4 Pro both achieved 100% precision (7/7 and 8/8 correct respectively, zero wrong flags) but recalls of only 17.50% and 20.00%; Claude Sonnet 4 and Qwen 3.5 each found 7/40 flags at 71.43% precision. A clear precision/recall trade-off emerged: conservative models achieved perfect precision but lower recall, while broader-exploring models (Gemini 3 Pro) achieved higher recall at the cost of some incorrect submissions -- no model excelled on both axes. Exploration Efficiency (Table 5) also diverged sharply: Gemini 3 Pro converted exploration into success far more effectively (EE=64.50%, RR=0.00%) than the other five models, which clustered at EE 12.50-22.58% (Sonnet 4 17.24%, GPT 5.2 17.50%, Qwen 3.5 12.50%, DeepSeek V4 Pro 21.05%, Opus 4.5 22.58%, RR 0.33-4.76%). Complexity analysis (Table 6) showed Claude Opus 4.5 used by far the fewest rounds (40.15) and was fastest (788.85 sec) while Gemini 3 Pro (315.25 rounds, 2380.98 sec) and Qwen 3.5 (346.08 rounds) explored much more broadly; average per-run cost ranged from ~$2.01 (DeepSeek V4 Pro) to $5.16 (Claude Opus 4.5). In the two hand-authored multi-stage case studies ("The Silent Corridor," built on CVE-2018-7600; "The Glass Atrium," a 3-flag/2-service chain built on CVE-2014-6271 and CVE-2017-9841), all six models found at least one valid flag with zero incorrect submissions, but coverage was the bottleneck: Claude Opus 4.5 and Gemini 3 Pro reached 2/5 flags while the other four models reached only 1/5, and all models resolved exactly 2/5 entry points, showing consistent difficulty completing the deeper internal-discovery/pivoting stages of a kill chain even when initial access succeeds.
core_contribution: The paper introduces CTFExplorer, a benchmark suite that shifts LLM offensive-security evaluation from isolated single-target CTF challenges to a multi-target setting. It deploys 40 web-based vulnerable services (each in its own Docker container) inside one shared environment so that agents must autonomously discover, distinguish, prioritize, and exploit targets without being told which service or vulnerability is in play. Alongside the benchmark, the authors present (1) CTFExplorer Agent, a reference multi-agent architecture with parallel entry-point exploration, a dispatcher/supervisor for knowledge hand-off between short-lived agents, budget-based self-reflection at 50%/80% checkpoints, and an LLM Critic that intervenes after three failed agents on an entry point; and (2) CTFExplorerEval, an agent-agnostic evaluation server exposed via the Model Context Protocol (MCP) that forces agents to label every submission with an exploration-level (L0 service identification through L4 demonstrated impact) and an evidence type (observation/hypothesis/finding/poc/impact), builds a live reasoning knowledge graph (including 'lateral edges' that capture cross-target/cross-port dependencies), and runs an LLM oracle at session end to score kill-chain-stage coverage against a reference writeup. Note: the record's title/abstract field ("CyberExplorer") does not match the paper's actual title and self-reported name, "CTFExplorer" -- the full PDF text consistently uses CTFExplorer throughout (title page, section headers, tables, figures).
threat_model: Offensive/red-team evaluation setting, not a defensive one: autonomous LLM agents act as attackers probing a partially observable, noisy multi-service host. Agents have no ground truth, no flags, and no writeups in advance; they interact only through a fixed MCP tool surface (run_command, submit_flag, create_file, giveup, get_graph, list_findings) and must infer via reconnaissance which of the 40 exposed ports/services are real targets versus distractors. The environment configuration includes decoy/distractor ports specifically to test target discrimination under uncertainty. This models a realistic multi-service host reconnaissance/triage scenario rather than a single pre-identified vulnerable service.
limitations: The paper's own discussion and design choices point to several limitations: (1) the benchmark is restricted to web-based challenges only, chosen because web is 'the most prevalent attack surface' and 'naturally suited to concurrent multi-service deployment' -- other attack surfaces (binary exploitation, network services, etc.) are explicitly out of scope; (2) the oracle-based kill-chain coverage evaluation depends on an LLM judge scoring against a reference writeup, introducing judge reliability as an unquantified factor; (3) results are single-run per model under fixed iteration/cost budgets, so no repeated-run variance/robustness analysis is reported in the main results (the authors list 'repeated-run robustness evaluation' as future work); (4) the reference CTFExplorer Agent architecture (parallel dispatch, supervisor hand-off, critic intervention) is only one possible multi-agent design and all six-model comparisons are confounded by this fixed scaffold rather than isolating pure model capability; (5) the two illustrative case studies (Silent Corridor, Glass Atrium) are small, hand-picked chains (single-digit flag counts) used for qualitative illustration, not statistically powered comparisons.

datasets_used: CTFExplorer benchmark: 40 web-based CTF challenges aggregated from NYU CTF Bench (9), HKCERT CTF (8), Project Sekai CTF (8), Hack The Box (7), CodeGate CTF (5), Google CTF (3)
Case study 'The Silent Corridor' (built on CVE-2018-7600)
Case study 'The Glass Atrium' (3-flag, 2-service chain built on CVE-2014-6271 and CVE-2017-9841)
quantitative_results: Flags found/40 (Table 4): Gemini 3 Pro 13 (11 correct/2 wrong), Claude Opus 4.5 8 (7/1), DeepSeek V4 Pro 8 (8/0), Claude Sonnet 4 7 (5/2), GPT 5.2 7 (7/0), Qwen 3.5 7 (5/2)
Precision/Recall (%): GPT 5.2 100.00/17.50, DeepSeek V4 Pro 100.00/20.00, Claude Opus 4.5 87.50/17.50, Gemini 3 Pro 84.62/27.50, Claude Sonnet 4 71.43/12.50, Qwen 3.5 71.43/12.50
Entry-points resolved/40: Gemini 3 Pro 40, GPT 5.2 40, Qwen 3.5 40, DeepSeek V4 Pro 40, Claude Opus 4.5 31, Claude Sonnet 4 29

Exploration Efficiency / Redundancy Rate (Table 5): Gemini 3 Pro 64.50%/0.00%, Claude Opus 4.5 22.58%/4.76%, DeepSeek V4 Pro 21.05%/0.00%, GPT 5.2 17.50%/0.33%, Claude Sonnet 4 17.24%/1.62%, Qwen 3.5 12.50%/0.66%

Case study results (Table 7): Claude Opus 4.5 2/5 flags (2/2 correct), Gemini 3 Pro 2/5 flags (2/2 correct); Claude Sonnet 4, GPT 5.2, Qwen 3.5, DeepSeek V4 Pro all 1/5 flags (1/1 correct); all models resolved 2/5 entry points, zero wrong flags across all models

Reasoning graph size (Appendix Table 8): GPT 5.2 largest with 1569 nodes/1529 edges; DeepSeek V4 Pro smallest with 120 nodes/44 edges; Gemini 3 Pro 169 nodes/109 edges; Claude Opus 4.5 497/416; Claude Sonnet 4 599/537; Qwen 3.5 362/164
baselines_compared: (compared via Table 1 feature checklist: Multi-Target, Target Agnostic, Autonomous Exploration, Strategic Reasoning, Behavioral Evaluation -- CTFExplorer is the only one scoring positive on all five)
